<insights>

- 23 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
- With 70 files and 23 entry points this is the largest and most central zone — it owns the schema (v1.ts, levels.ts, index.ts) as well as the full analyze pipeline, making it a high-leverage area where changes have broad impact.
- The bidirectional imports with both the cli zone (30 imports in, 54 out) and the core zone (18 imports in, 67 out) indicate this zone is deeply embedded in the cross-package call graph; any refactor here cascades widely.
- Cohesion of 0.55 is acceptable given the zone's breadth, but the schema types (src/schema/) could be extracted into a dedicated schema zone to reduce the surface area and improve cohesion of both.
- The schema files (v1.ts, levels.ts, index.ts) are bundled with analyze pipeline logic — extracting them to a dedicated schema zone would improve cohesion and make the type contract more auditable.
- Bidirectional coupling with cli (unit-analyze → cli: 30 imports; cli → unit-analyze: 54 imports) creates a tight circular dependency risk — verify that no analyze module imports a CLI command module directly.
- Zone "prd-analyze-core" has files across 10 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
- prd-analyze-core imports from store (7 imports: unit-analyze→store): analyze pipeline logic should return results to callers, not read or write storage directly — this is a separation-of-concerns violation that ties the analyze domain to the persistence layer.
- prd-analyze-core is imported by every non-trivial zone (core, cli, store, unit, unit-core, e2e) while simultaneously importing back from cli, core, and store — it is structurally impossible to isolate, test, or replace this zone without touching the entire package.
- prd-analyze-core → store (7 imports): the analyze pipeline accesses storage directly, creating a dependency from a pure domain layer to the persistence layer. Analyze logic should pass results to callers who decide what to persist — this is a layering inversion.
- prd-analyze-core is the only zone imported by all six other non-trivial zones simultaneously while also importing back from three of them. Its de facto role as a foundation layer is structurally incompatible with its position as a mid-tier domain zone — it should either be explicitly promoted to the foundation tier or split so shared primitives live below it.
- 23 exported entry points with no enforced public/private boundary means consumers across all 6 importing zones routinely bind to internal helpers; any internal refactor is a de facto breaking change regardless of intent.
- src/schema/index.ts living inside prd-analyze-core forces every zone that needs only schema types to take a compile-time dependency on the full analyze pipeline — type-only consumers are unnecessarily coupled to pipeline churn.
- The 7 outbound imports to store (unit-analyze→store: 7) combined with 20 inbound from store (store→unit-analyze: 20) confirm a bidirectional persistence dependency: analyze logic both reads from and is consumed by the storage layer, eliminating any possibility of testing analyze logic without a real store.
- src/schema/index.ts (hub: imported by 7 zones) is bundled inside prd-analyze-core alongside analyze pipeline logic. Schema types are structural contracts that belong in a foundation layer; colocating them with pipeline code forces all 7 consumers to take a transitive dependency on the analyze pipeline to access types.
- 23 public entry points with no enforced boundary between public API and internal helpers allow consumers to import arbitrarily deep into the zone's internals. This makes the effective API surface equal to the zone's full file tree, not its intended contract.
- The zone has 23 declared entry points but the subpath exports wildcard (dist/*) means the effective API surface is all 70 files. The 23-entry-point count creates a false sense of governance: consumers see a number implying a controlled API but face no enforcement. This gap between declared and actual API surface is more misleading than having no declared entry points at all, because it suggests boundary discipline exists when none is enforced.
- Replace the dist/* wildcard export in package.json with the 23 explicit named entry points so that any consumer importing an undeclared path receives a compile-time error rather than silently accessing internals. The declared-but-unenforced boundary is actively misleading — enforcement converts the advisory list into an actual contract.
- [call graph] 2743 internal calls, 291 outgoing, 208 incoming (cohesion: 0.9, coupling: 0.1)

</insights>
